_comment: Cross-domain conversions: non-combat progression turning into character power. Each entry reads as 'per level of <source>, add <per_level> to <target> as a <class> modifier'. Sources are a non_combat_skill (by name) or floor_depth (the floor the player is on). Classes are the same three the stat pipeline uses everywhere else: flat, increased (percentages sum into one pool), more (each source multiplies separately).
_constraint: Conversions may only target UTILITY stats, never combat stats. This is enforced at load in gep/crossdomain.py, not left to reviewer discipline: if mining raised damage, mining would become a prerequisite for raiding no matter how it were framed. Targets currently wireable: gather_yield, gather_speed, item_rarity, move_speed. Declared but refused until a system reads them: craft_quality, resource_tier_access.
_tuning: Every per_level below is a placeholder shape, not a balance pass. At 0.5 increased per level a level-99 miner gathers +49.5% ore, which is deliberately noticeable rather than tuned.

conversions._note: A miner pulls more from a vein. The obvious one, and the reason the seam exists.
Foraging and aboriculture work the same nodes from the other end -- they speed the swing rather than deepen the haul, so specialising in one is not strictly worse than the other.
aboriculture, spelled as skills.json spells it.
Craftsmanship reads the market rather than the rock: it improves what drops, not what is dug.
Depth is the one non-skill source. Descending is progression too, and it should pay in find rather than in power -- this is what makes a deep floor worth farming when its bases are no better.
Farming is stamina work. Sideways into traversal, which is utility with no combat edge.
